body:
I see. Ya so HF (last time I checked) didn't easily support stop sequences. So one way around this is something like

```
response = manifest.run(prompt, **kwargs, return_response=True)
if stop:
    # Assuming the goal is to get the first stop token
    shortest_ans = sorted([response.get_response(st) for st in stop], key=len)
    ans = shortest_ans[0]
else:
    ans = response.get_response()
return ans
```

body:
> (medium) how did you come up the versions in tool.poetry.dependencies? i think a few could be more permissive - happy to point out which ones, but just wondering what method you used to get those

The very naive leave-the-version-blank in the `poetry init` questionnaire 😛 . Happy to try another method for finding permissive ranges, or loosening any you suggest.
author: shoelsch

body:
> (small) possible to also support 3.7 until its EOL? im guessing maybe the reason you didn't is manifest-ml doesnt?

`numpy` and `flake8` also have dropped support for 3.7, the former end of last year and the latter just recently.

Happy to loosen these constraints to find a compatible, older version.

For `manifest-ml`, I think we can include it as an optional dependency for a specific version of Python, but not 100% sure yet.
author: shoelsch

body: oh wow i didnt know numpy did. okay im fine moving on
author: hwchase17

body: i think numpy is the one im most concerned about, because i know a lot of other machine learning packages have that as a requirement and often are more closely tied to specific versions (its been a pain in the past). maybe we can lower that to 1.22.0? that was the version released nearly a year ago. i think we could probably go lower, but can wait to see if anyone complains
author: hwchase17

body:
okay im taking a look now. my biggest thought is around the interface for __call__ on the LLM class. right now, __call__ is really simplistic in that it just returns the simplest possible thing (the most likely string). however, this is not really the FULL output of the LLM - the full output would be the N most likely things, as well as other things that ive been meaning to add in like log probs

i would love to (1) define what a full, complete response would look like (N most likely things, logprobs, anything else?), (2) define a separate method on the LLM that returns this (the reason i dont want to change the current __call__ is for a combination of backwards compability + ease of use - i do think the single string return is useful in enough place where its handy to keep it around
author: hwchase17

body:
i would do something like
```
@classmethod
def from_model_id(model_id: str, task: Optional[str] = None, model_kwargs: Optional[dict] = None, **kwargs):
    # construct pipeline here
    return cls(pipeline=pipeline, **kwargs)
```

this allows users to do either

`HuggingFacePipeline(pipeline=pipeline)` or `HuggingFacePipeline.from_model_id("gpt2")`

i think if we do this, we can also just delete the above validate_environment call

i think we also no longer need to save the model_id, model_kwargs, and task param on the class (which simplifies the interface imensely)

body: Yeah, I went back and forth on this but ultimately decided it's fine to use either kwargs or args for multiple input keys. If not using kwargs, user has to pay attn to ordering of input keys. People have to do the same thing for function args too. Happy to restrict to one arg if you feel strongly
author: agola11

body:
the difference in my mind is that function args are pretty easily inspectable/obvious (eg you can shift tab, command p, etc) and that if you mess up the order its possible/likely that some other error would occur (due to type differences, etc)

here the order is not obvious, nad there would  likely never be a loud error (since boht mostly type string) so it would silently mess it up... i feel medium strong about this

body: This seems to result in the final answer when a tool is used to come formatted as `Final Answer: ARR yesterday was 3500.0.`. Which then fails in `_extract_tool_and_input` as it expects something like `AI: ARR yesterday was 3500.0.` From my testing it can be fixed by customising `FORMAT_INSTRUCTIONS` to end in `AI: the final answer to the original input question`

When calling set_callback_manager on AgentExecutor, should it propagate to the inner objects? ie. to avoid code like this

```py
self.agent_executor.callback_manager = callback_manager
self.agent_executor.agent.llm_chain.callback_manager = callback_manager
self.agent_executor.agent.llm_chain.llm.callback_manager = callback_manager
```
author: nfcampos

body: There's a case to be made for these not being abstract methods and instead having a default implementation of `pass` for all methods. Would make writing custom handlers a lot shorter, and I don't see much downside
author: nfcampos

body: I wonder if this is worth the trouble? I get what it's for, but maybe a more useful pattern might be to have a SharedCallbackManager that always executes the handlers on the main thread (via the asyncio event loop). Thoughts?

body: yeah fair point - i think this is a special enough message that we can make it its own method. `on_agent_end` seem good?
author: hwchase17

body: yes, but i think it should only be called once (in langchain.__init__ when first loading the library)
author: hwchase17

body: In python any function can be imported by a user of your module, therefore users of langchain can call it multiple times

My preference would be to wait till tracing is released (a follow-up to this PR) before changing the way global callback manager works (execute all handlers on main thread); we have something working right now that will require some moderate-high changes to get it to work with asyncio. Happy to have this conversation in a couple of weeks
author: agola11

body:
It does get passed in to the child `LLMChain` but not the `LLMChain`'s `LLM`:

```
    manager = CallbackManager([handler])
    tool = "Search"
    responses = [
        f"FooBarBaz\nAction: {tool}\nAction Input: misalignment",
        "Oh well\nAction: Final Answer\nAction Input: curses foiled again",
    ]
    fake_llm = FakeListLLM(responses=responses, callback_manager=manager, verbose=True)
    tools = [
        Tool("Search", lambda x: x, "Useful for searching"),
    ]
    agent = initialize_agent(
        tools,
        fake_llm,
        agent="zero-shot-react-description",
        verbose=True,
        callback_manager=manager,
    )
```

^ this is the pattern you'd have to follow to get the callback manager to all children modules of the `AgentExecutor` (pass in `CallbackManager` to `LLM`) essentially.

see here for more details: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/db5c8e0c42d9c38ea1373f1c0355698527350e61/langchain/agents/agent.py#L135
author: agola11

body: yeah lets wait for now
author: hwchase17

body:
Yes that pattern works fine when initialising, it's just annoying when you have to change the callback manager across the board for an existing agent that you don't want to throw away (because eg you want to keep memory)

ie. something like this

```py
class MyLangchainAgent:
    agent_executor: AgentExecutor
    other_langchain_objects: list[Chain | BaseLLM] = []

    def __init__(self, state: dict, loop: asyncio.AbstractEventLoop):
        super().__init__(state, loop)

        llm = OpenAI(temperature=0, verbose=True)
        llm_math_chain = LLMMathChain(llm=llm, verbose=True)
        tools = [
            Tool(
                name="Calculator",
                func=llm_math_chain.run,
                description="useful for when you need to answer questions about math",
            ),
        ]
        self.agent_executor = initialize_agent(
            tools, llm, agent="zero-shot-react-description", verbose=True
        )
        self.langchain_objects = [llm_math_chain]

    async def __call__(self, user_message: dict, ctx: RunContext):
        # Set up callback manager for the run
        callback_manager = CallbackManager([ConcatCallbackHandler(ctx)])
        for obj in self.other_langchain_objects + [
            self.agent_executor,
            self.agent_executor.agent.llm_chain,
            self.agent_executor.agent.llm_chain.llm,
        ]:
            obj.callback_manager = callback_manager

        # Run the agent
        await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(
            None, self.agent_executor.run, "".join(user_message["content_parts"])
        )
```
author: nfcampos

body:
@nickscamara can we return more from the Wolfram response (e.g. the units of the answer)? For example, if you run a query like "How big is the moon?" via LangChain's wrapper, you get back "1079.6", and then LangChain will return "The moon is 1079.6 miles in diameter.", which is incorrect - 1079.6 is actually the *radius*, not the diameter. 

I'm assuming the Wolfram response included that, but it was just dropped by the wrapper?
author: bborn

body: Thank you for that! #584 should now include the assumption.
author: nickscamara

body: i'd also imagine this is a pretty common use case, could be worth adding it as an abstractmethod in the base VectorStore class
